subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Noting in Default — When Available and How Obtained — r.19.01
fact_pattern: The plaintiff served the statement of claim on the defendant on January 10. Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, the defendant was required to deliver a statement of defence within 20 days of service. By February 5 — well past the deadline — the defendant has not filed a defence or appeared in the proceeding. The plaintiff's counsel asks how to proceed.
call_of_question: Under r.19.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how does a plaintiff note a defendant in default, and what are the procedural requirements?

options.A: To note a defendant in default, the plaintiff must bring a motion on notice to the defendant, proving both service and the failure to deliver a defence; a court order is required.
options.B: Under r.19.01, a plaintiff may note a defendant in default by filing a requisition with the registrar once the time for delivering a defence has expired; the registrar notes the defendant in default without a court order, provided proof of service and the affidavit of default are filed.
options.C: A defendant may only be noted in default if the plaintiff first sends a demand letter by registered mail and waits an additional 30 days after that demand.
options.D: Noting in default requires an order from a judge and cannot be obtained from the registrar.

explanation: Under r.19.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where a defendant fails to deliver a statement of defence within the time prescribed, the plaintiff may note the defendant in default. The process is administrative: the plaintiff files a requisition with the registrar, accompanied by proof of service of the statement of claim and an affidavit confirming that the time for delivering a defence has expired and no defence has been delivered. The registrar then notes the defendant in default. No motion before a judge and no prior demand letter are required. Once noted in default, the defendant's right to participate in the proceeding is restricted.
why_A_wrong: Noting in default does not require a motion before a judge. It is an administrative process obtained from the registrar upon filing the requisition and supporting materials.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 19.01 permits noting in default administratively through the registrar upon filing proof of service and affidavit of default; no court order is required.
why_C_wrong: There is no requirement to send a demand letter and wait 30 additional days before noting in default. Once the prescribed time for delivering a defence has expired, the plaintiff may proceed to note the defendant in default.
why_D_wrong: Noting in default does not require a judge's order. The registrar notes the defendant in default administratively upon receipt of the required materials.

source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.01

tested_concepts: r.19.01 — noting in default is administrative registrar process
no motion or court order required
proof of service and affidavit of default required

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Effect of Noting in Default — Defendant's Rights
fact_pattern: After the plaintiff notes the defendant in default, the defendant's newly-retained counsel contacts the plaintiff's lawyer and asks to be served with all subsequent documents in the proceeding and to participate in any motion for default judgment. The plaintiff's counsel argues that a defendant noted in default has no further rights in the proceeding.
call_of_question: Under r.19.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what rights does a defendant noted in default retain in the proceeding?

options.A: A defendant noted in default has no further rights whatsoever in the proceeding; the defendant cannot participate in any way until the noting is set aside.
options.B: Under r.19.02, a defendant noted in default may not take any further steps in the proceeding except to move to set aside the noting in default or to participate in a reference or assessment of damages; the defendant is entitled to receive notice of and participate in any motion for default judgment.
options.C: A defendant noted in default may continue to file pleadings and participate in the proceeding as if no default had occurred; the noting in default is merely a procedural formality.
options.D: A defendant noted in default is entitled to participate fully in all steps of the proceeding including filing a statement of defence; the noting merely places the defendant on record as being in default.

explanation: Under r.19.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who has been noted in default may not take any further steps in the proceeding without first obtaining an order setting aside the noting in default — except for specific permitted steps. The defendant may: (1) move to set aside the noting in default under r.19.08; and (2) attend and participate in any motion for default judgment to contest the quantum of damages, including on a reference for the assessment of damages. The defendant is entitled to notice of a motion for default judgment. The plaintiff's counsel's assertion that a defendant noted in default has no rights is incorrect; the defendant may contest the quantum of damages even while in default.
why_A_wrong: A defendant noted in default does retain some rights. The defendant may move to set aside the noting and may participate in the assessment of damages on any motion for default judgment.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.19.02, a defendant noted in default cannot take most procedural steps but retains the right to contest damages and move to set aside the noting.
why_C_wrong: A defendant noted in default cannot continue to participate as if nothing happened. The noting in default significantly restricts the defendant's ability to participate in the proceeding; leave to file a defence or take other steps requires setting aside the default.
why_D_wrong: A defendant noted in default cannot simply file a statement of defence. Any defence filed after noting in default requires either an order setting aside the noting, or leave to defend. The defendant's procedural rights are substantially curtailed.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Effect of Noting in Default — Defendant's Rights
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.02

tested_concepts: r.19.02 — defendant noted in default restricted from most steps
defendant may participate in damages assessment
defendant entitled to notice of default judgment motion

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Default Judgment — Liquidated Demand as of Right — r.19.04
fact_pattern: The plaintiff's action is for $85,000 in unpaid invoices — a specific, fixed sum with a precise amount owed. The defendant is noted in default. The plaintiff's counsel asks whether a default judgment may be obtained without a motion before a judge.
call_of_question: Under r.19.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when may a plaintiff obtain default judgment as of right without a motion, and what type of claim qualifies?

options.A: Default judgment may always be obtained as of right from the registrar once the defendant is noted in default; no motion is ever required.
options.B: Default judgment may be obtained as of right from the registrar under r.19.04 where the claim is for a liquidated demand — a specific amount of money that is fixed and ascertainable from the documents without the need for a trial or reference; the registrar signs judgment in the amount claimed.
options.C: Default judgment may only be obtained by motion before a judge, even for liquidated demands; the registrar has no authority to sign judgment.
options.D: Default judgment is not available until 60 days after the defendant is noted in default; a waiting period is required before any default judgment can be obtained.

explanation: Under r.19.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where a defendant has been noted in default and the plaintiff's claim is for a liquidated demand — meaning a specific sum of money that is certain or readily ascertainable from the plaintiff's documents, such as an unpaid invoice for a fixed amount — the plaintiff may obtain default judgment as of right from the registrar without a motion before a judge. The registrar signs the judgment in the amount claimed, plus costs and post-judgment interest. A claim for unpaid invoices of $85,000 is a paradigm example of a liquidated demand. No judicial hearing is required for the registrar to sign such a judgment.
why_A_wrong: Default judgment is not available as of right in all cases. Only liquidated demands permit the registrar to sign judgment without a motion; unliquidated claims (such as general damages) require a motion before a judge.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 19.04 permits the registrar to sign default judgment for liquidated demands without a motion; unpaid invoices are a classic liquidated demand.
why_C_wrong: For liquidated demands, a motion before a judge is not required. The registrar has express authority under r.19.04 to sign judgment in such cases.
why_D_wrong: There is no mandatory 60-day waiting period before obtaining default judgment. Once the defendant is noted in default, the plaintiff may proceed to obtain judgment (for liquidated demands, from the registrar) without any waiting period beyond the expiry of the time for filing a defence.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Default Judgment — Liquidated Demand as of Right — r.19.04
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.04

tested_concepts: r.19.04 — default judgment for liquidated demand from registrar as of right
liquidated demand — specific fixed ascertainable amount
no motion required for liquidated demand default judgment

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Default Judgment — Unliquidated Claim by Motion — r.19.05
fact_pattern: The plaintiff in a personal injury action notes the defendant in default. The plaintiff's claim is for general damages, pain and suffering, and loss of income — all of which are unliquidated. The plaintiff asks whether default judgment can be obtained from the registrar.
call_of_question: Under r.19.05 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how does a plaintiff obtain default judgment where the claim is for an unliquidated amount?

options.A: The plaintiff may obtain default judgment for unliquidated claims from the registrar in the same way as liquidated claims; no distinction is made.
options.B: For unliquidated claims, the plaintiff must bring a motion before a judge to obtain default judgment; the judge may assess the damages on the motion or direct a reference for assessment of damages.
options.C: Unliquidated claims are not eligible for default judgment; the plaintiff must proceed to a full trial even where the defendant is in default.
options.D: For unliquidated claims, the defendant must be given another 30 days to file a defence before default judgment may be sought on a motion.

explanation: Under r.19.05 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where a defendant has been noted in default and the plaintiff's claim is for an unliquidated amount — meaning a claim for damages that requires assessment, such as general damages, pain and suffering, or loss of income — the plaintiff must bring a motion before a judge to obtain default judgment. The judge may: (1) assess the damages on the motion; or (2) direct a reference to an assessment officer or master to assess the damages. The defendant, though in default, is entitled to notice of the motion and may participate in the assessment of damages to challenge the quantum. Default judgment for unliquidated claims is not available from the registrar as of right.
why_A_wrong: Unliquidated claims cannot be resolved by the registrar signing default judgment as of right. The distinction between liquidated and unliquidated claims is fundamental to the default judgment process under rr.19.04 and 19.05.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.19.05, unliquidated claims require a motion before a judge, who may assess damages on the motion or direct a reference for assessment.
why_C_wrong: Unliquidated claims are eligible for default judgment; the plaintiff is not required to proceed to a full trial. A motion under r.19.05 provides the mechanism for obtaining judgment on unliquidated claims.
why_D_wrong: There is no additional 30-day period before the plaintiff may bring a motion for default judgment on an unliquidated claim. Once the defendant is noted in default, the motion may be brought.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Default Judgment — Unliquidated Claim by Motion — r.19.05
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.05

tested_concepts: r.19.05 — unliquidated claims require motion before judge
judge may assess damages or direct reference
defendant entitled to notice and may contest quantum

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Defence Filed Late After Noting But Before Judgment
fact_pattern: The plaintiff notes the defendant in default. Three days after being noted in default — and before any default judgment is obtained — the defendant delivers a statement of defence. The plaintiff argues that the late-delivered defence should be disregarded because the defendant is already in default and the plaintiff intends to proceed to default judgment.
call_of_question: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, may a defendant deliver a statement of defence after being noted in default but before default judgment is obtained, and what effect does this have?

options.A: A defendant noted in default may never file a statement of defence without first obtaining an order setting aside the noting; the late defence is a nullity and must be struck.
options.B: Under r.19.03, a defendant noted in default who delivers a statement of defence after noting but before default judgment is obtained may do so; the delivery of the defence after noting in default does not automatically set aside the noting, but the plaintiff may not obtain default judgment if a defence has been filed. The defendant may need to obtain an order setting aside the noting to regularize the proceeding.
options.C: A defendant noted in default may file a defence at any time and the filing automatically sets aside the noting in default; no court order is required.
options.D: Once noted in default, the defendant is permanently barred from delivering any defence and the only recourse is to move to set aside the default judgment after it is obtained.

explanation: Under r.19.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a defendant who has been noted in default may, with the consent of the plaintiff's counsel or leave of the court, deliver a statement of defence at any time before judgment has been obtained. In practice, where a defendant delivers a defence after being noted in default but before judgment, the plaintiff may accept the defence (in effect setting aside the noting by conduct) or may bring a motion to strike the late defence, or the defendant may move to have the noting set aside under r.19.08 to regularize the proceeding. The filing of a defence alone does not automatically set aside the noting in default; the noting remains until formally set aside. However, the plaintiff typically cannot obtain default judgment while a defence has been delivered.
why_A_wrong: A defendant noted in default may, with leave or consent, deliver a defence before judgment is obtained. The rules do not permanently bar a late defence; r.19.03 contemplates this situation.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A defendant may deliver a defence after noting but before judgment; this does not automatically set aside the noting, but the plaintiff typically cannot obtain default judgment while a defence is on file.
why_C_wrong: Filing a defence after noting in default does not automatically set aside the noting. The noting remains in effect until formally set aside by court order or by the parties' consent. The filing of a defence simply places the defence on the record and may prompt a motion to regularize the situation.
why_D_wrong: The defendant is not permanently barred from delivering any defence once noted in default. Rule 19.03 contemplates delivery of a defence with leave or consent before judgment is obtained; the defendant need not wait until after default judgment to take action.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Defence Filed Late After Noting But Before Judgment
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.03

tested_concepts: r.19.03 — defence may be filed with leave/consent after noting but before judgment
filing does not automatically set aside the noting
plaintiff cannot obtain default judgment while defence on file

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment — r.19.08 Test
fact_pattern: Default judgment was obtained against the defendant for $90,000. The defendant brings a motion under r.19.08 to set aside the default judgment. The defendant explains that the default occurred because the defendant changed addresses and did not receive the claim. The defendant also provides an affidavit from a lawyer outlining a potential defence to the action on the merits.
call_of_question: Under r.19.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what is the test for setting aside a default judgment?

options.A: A default judgment will be set aside if the defendant can show any reason for the default, however weak; the courts favour allowing cases to be decided on their merits.
options.B: Under r.19.08, a court may set aside a default judgment on such terms as are just; the court generally considers whether the defendant has provided a reasonable explanation for the default and whether there is a prima facie defence on the merits — a meritorious defence.
options.C: A default judgment can only be set aside if the plaintiff's counsel consents to setting it aside; the defendant has no right to bring a motion without the plaintiff's consent.
options.D: A default judgment will be set aside automatically upon the defendant filing an affidavit explaining the reason for the default; no court order is required.

explanation: Under r.19.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a court may set aside or vary a default judgment on such terms as are just. The court exercises a broad discretion. The established two-part test requires the defendant to demonstrate: (1) a reasonable explanation for the failure to file a defence (i.e., a satisfactory reason for the default); and (2) a prima facie defence on the merits — meaning the defendant has at least an arguable case or a defence that is not bound to fail. Courts also consider the promptness with which the defendant moved to set aside the judgment, and any prejudice to the plaintiff from setting it aside. Failure to establish either element may result in the motion being dismissed.
why_A_wrong: The test is not as lenient as accepting any reason for the default. The defendant must show both a reasonable explanation and a prima facie meritorious defence; a trivial or inadequate explanation will not suffice.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.19.08, the court considers both a reasonable explanation for the default and a prima facie defence on the merits; the court has discretion to impose terms on setting aside.
why_C_wrong: A motion to set aside default judgment does not require the plaintiff's consent. The defendant may bring the motion; the plaintiff may oppose it and the court decides based on the applicable test.
why_D_wrong: Setting aside a default judgment requires a court order under r.19.08; it is not automatic upon filing an affidavit. The court exercises discretion and the defendant must satisfy the two-part test.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment — r.19.08 Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.08

tested_concepts: r.19.08 — two-part test: reasonable explanation + prima facie defence on merits
court exercises discretion and may impose terms
promptness and prejudice also considered

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Setting Aside Default — Costs Imposed and Terms
fact_pattern: The court sets aside a default judgment against the defendant under r.19.08. The defendant is now permitted to file a defence and the action proceeds. The plaintiff's counsel argues that costs should be imposed on the defendant as a condition of setting aside the default judgment, as the plaintiff was put to the expense of obtaining the judgment and now of having it set aside.
call_of_question: When a court sets aside a default judgment under r.19.08, is it common to impose costs on the defendant as a term of the order, and what other terms may the court impose?

options.A: No — when a default judgment is set aside, the plaintiff automatically bears all costs of the default proceedings; the defendant pays nothing.
options.B: Yes — it is common practice for courts to impose costs on the defendant as a condition of setting aside the default judgment; other terms may include requiring the defendant to deliver a statement of defence within a specified time, pay an amount into court, or provide security. The terms are designed to compensate the plaintiff for the prejudice caused by the defendant's default.
options.C: The court has no power to impose any conditions when setting aside a default judgment; the set-aside must be unconditional if the defendant satisfies the test.
options.D: Costs may only be imposed on the defendant if the plaintiff can prove the default was deliberate; inadvertent defaults result in no costs consequences for the defendant.

explanation: Under r.19.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court may set aside or vary a default judgment on 'such terms as are just.' This phrase gives the court broad discretion to impose conditions when setting aside the default judgment. It is common practice in Ontario for courts to impose costs on the defendant as a condition of setting aside the judgment — typically costs of the default proceedings including the cost of obtaining the judgment and the motion to set it aside. Additionally, the court may impose other terms such as: requiring the defendant to file a statement of defence within a specified time; paying a sum into court as security; or other conditions that protect the plaintiff against the prejudice caused by the default. A defendant who satisfies the r.19.08 test is not automatically entitled to an unconditional set-aside.
why_A_wrong: The plaintiff does not automatically bear all costs of the default proceedings upon set-aside. Courts routinely require the defendant — whose default caused the proceedings — to pay the plaintiff's costs as a condition of the order.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Courts impose costs and other terms on defendants as conditions of setting aside default judgments; terms may include costs, a defence deadline, and security.
why_C_wrong: The court has broad power to impose conditions under r.19.08. The phrase 'on such terms as are just' expressly authorizes conditional set-asides; an unconditional set-aside is not required.
why_D_wrong: Costs are commonly imposed on defendants even where the default was inadvertent. The purpose is to compensate the plaintiff for the costs caused by the default, not to punish deliberate misconduct.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Setting Aside Default — Costs Imposed and Terms
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.08

tested_concepts: r.19.08 — set-aside on such terms as are just — broad discretion
common to impose costs on defendant as condition
other terms: defence deadline, security, payment into court

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Setting Aside Noting vs Setting Aside Judgment — Distinct Steps
fact_pattern: A defendant has been noted in default and default judgment has subsequently been obtained against it. The defendant brings a motion to 'set aside the default.' The defendant assumes that setting aside the default judgment will automatically set aside the noting in default, allowing it to file a defence. The plaintiff argues that setting aside the judgment and setting aside the noting are two distinct steps.
call_of_question: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, are setting aside a default judgment and setting aside a noting in default the same step, or distinct procedural steps?

options.A: They are the same step; a court order setting aside the default judgment automatically and simultaneously sets aside the noting in default and entitles the defendant to file a defence.
options.B: Setting aside a default judgment and setting aside the noting in default are distinct procedural steps; an order setting aside the default judgment alone does not automatically set aside the noting in default. The defendant must also obtain an order setting aside the noting in default (or the court must expressly set aside both in the same order) before the defendant is entitled to file a statement of defence and participate in the proceeding.
options.C: A defendant can file a defence as soon as the default judgment is set aside, without any need to address the noting in default.
options.D: Once judgment is obtained, the noting in default merges into the judgment and disappears; setting aside the judgment is the only step required and automatically restores all of the defendant's procedural rights.

explanation: Setting aside a default judgment under r.19.08 and setting aside the noting in default under r.19.08 are distinct procedural steps that address different aspects of the default. A default judgment is the final order obtained after noting in default; the noting in default is the earlier administrative step. When a court sets aside a default judgment, this removes the final order, but it does not automatically restore the defendant to the position of a defendant who has not been noted in default. The defendant remains noted in default, which restricts the defendant from filing a defence or taking other procedural steps. To regularize the proceeding and allow the defendant to deliver a defence, the court must also set aside the noting in default — either in the same order or by a separate order. Courts often address both in the same motion, but counsel must ensure both are expressly addressed.
why_A_wrong: Setting aside the default judgment alone does not automatically set aside the noting in default. These are distinct procedural steps and the order setting aside the judgment must expressly (or by necessary implication) also set aside the noting for the defendant's full rights to be restored.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Setting aside the judgment and setting aside the noting are distinct steps; the defendant must ensure the noting is also addressed before it can file a defence and participate fully.
why_C_wrong: The defendant cannot simply file a defence after the judgment is set aside without also addressing the noting in default. The noting continues to restrict the defendant's procedural rights until it too is set aside.
why_D_wrong: The noting in default does not merge into or disappear upon the obtaining of judgment. It is a separate procedural step that has its own existence and must be addressed independently when seeking to restore the defendant's rights.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Setting Aside Noting vs Setting Aside Judgment — Distinct Steps
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.19.08

tested_concepts: setting aside judgment and setting aside noting are distinct steps
judgment set-aside does not automatically set aside noting in default
court must address both for defendant to file defence
